However, no evidence supports the use of an alternative figure, as a result would likely require further assumptions to justify.  4.4 Cost Benefit Analysis conclusion  The Cost Benefit figures show a positive return on expenditure on the societal perspective, but lower return on exchequer. Given that the maximum amount of time on ESF is three years, it might be that three years after an individual starts on ESF may not be sufficient time to receive the intervention and gain employment. These individuals may have achieved intermediate outcomes, but not yet employment particularly if they were significantly hard to help.  Many ESF participants faces multiple disadvantages, and for much of ESF provision having additional “barriers”, is a pre-requisite for being on the programme. In general, harder to help individuals do not generate significant returns particularly on the exchequer perspective, and if we only track outcomes for a small number of years. This is common across many labour market programmes. It could be the time horizon as previously mentioned or may be because the unit costs required to help those who have more barriers are relatively high and would need longer periods in employment to produce a positive return on investment.  Another factor is that for some of these individuals they will achieve other outcomes that are not monetised. For example, alcohol and drug issues are relatively common across some ESF participants yet we do not account for the health or crime savings from dealing with these issues as part of the CBA.  Furthermore, as stated previously a significant proportion of ESF participants are in employment upon starting. Roughly 1/3 of all expenditure is on people that are not inactive or unemployed upon starting. We would not expect the impacts of ESF to be on additional time in employment, or off benefits, but in higher wages and progression which these figures do not measure.  This is not an exhaustive list. However, it should be noted that despite all those caveats the CBRs are encouraging. The societal benefit is greater than £1 across all groups at the 36-month point and across all participants from the 24-month point onwards. The increase in the exchequer benefits suggest that the programme would generate enough exchequer benefits – on this relatively narrow definition of just DWP exchequer benefits – for all groups once a 48-month tracking period has been observed. It is also the case that it does at the 36-month point for the harder to help groups within this analysis. These are all positive outcomes from this work.  Chapter 5: Conclusions  5.1 Impact Analysis  The analysis uses an average treatment effect on the treated (ATT) to estimate the impact of ESF on participants. It uses a Propensity Score Matching (PSM) approach to compare the outcomes of those who went on ESF with those of a similar group who did not. Both groups were on benefits at the point of starting, in the case of participants, and pseudo-start, in the case of those that are not participants.  PSM approaches use a large amount of data on participant characteristics to control for various demographic traits that potentially influence labour market outcomes outside of ESF. The aim of the approach is to estimate the direct impact of the ESF programme.  The analysis splits participants into groups based on the type of benefits they are claiming on starting, whether they are unemployed, employed or inactive. This helps to account for the likely differences in labour market regime that occurs between groups.  The data used allows us to measure the number of additional days in employment that accrue at the three-year point, post start on ESF. For the control group this is measured by assigning the pseudo-start date, based on a distribution of their benefit start dates. This is the basis of the central estimate.  Our central case produces an estimate on the amount of additional time spent on active benefits, inactive benefits and employment. The primary outcome, however, is employment – which we use for the Cost Benefit Analysis (CBA). Whilst there is variation by benefit type upon starting, the central figure is that ESF participants spend around 40 days more in employment than similar non-participants.  A significant amount of sensitivity analysis has been carried out to ensure these estimates are robust as possible. In conjunction with the academics named at the start of the report we have altered the regression running both a probit and logit approach, changed the callipers and other technical elements that are not presented in this report. We have also shown the potential range of outcomes in the form of the confidence intervals that we do include.  Also presented in this analysis are estimates that show the various impacts on different types of outcomes, subset by the different benefit groups upon starting. Each show that the treatment group has a similar outcome over and above the comparison group. The results show the ESF is more likely to move inactive participants away from inactive benefits and subsequently into employment.  The ESF programme is voluntary. Whilst we do control for lots of observed characteristics and attempt to do so for unobserved, there could be further factors like motivation that may have the effect of creating bias in the results. However, there are also several outcomes that we know providers deliver which are not estimated in this analysis which may offset this.  ESF begins to deliver impacts within the first year, despite some people still being on the programme at that point. Outcomes build over time for all participants, in each of the main analysis groups. People start to move into employment and, for those that don’t, many move onto “active” benefits which, in the longer term, should mean they are more likely to gain employment in the future.  5.2 Cost Benefit Analysis  Using the impact analysis results, a Cost Benefit Analysis was undertaken to show the cost effectiveness of ESF over time. Primarily this was done at three years, but also at one and two years.  The analysis looks at the cost effectiveness from the exchequer and societal perspectives, as each value costs and benefits in a slightly different way. At the three-year point, for every pound invested:   The exchequer benefits by £0.69 Society benefits by £1.50   Sensitivity analysis on the Exchequer and society perspectives show that these results are consistent across various assumptions. The exchequer costs include all wages paid to participants, whereas society only includes these as a transfer payment. This is a large portion of the cost, so this discrepancy leads to a significant difference in results between these perspectives.  If we look at subgroups, for every pound invested:  Those who are employed on starting   £0.55 in exchequer benefits £1.23 in society benefits   Those who are unemployed on starting   £0.60 in exchequer benefits £1.30 in society benefits   Those who are inactive on starting   £1.17 in exchequer benefits £2.68 in society benefits   It is worth observing that even at the three-year point, the impact estimate graphs are not zero. This means that even after three years, whilst there is some expected decay, the positive results are on-going. This would give us reasonable confidence that the impacts of ESF carry on after three years. Given that the costs would be fixed, any further additional time in employment would generate more benefits at the same cost and therefore improve the cost benefit ratios.  It is also the case that whilst not exactly alike, these results are broadly in line with the YEI impact estimates and CBA figures, from the published report.[footnote 23] The main difference being that impacts were only tracked for 12 months, compared to 36 therefore not as many will have accrued, potentially limiting the amount of benefit observed.  It is important to reiterate that the accuracy of any cost and benefit estimates is very much dependent on the robustness of the impact estimates from which they are derived and the validity of the assumptions upon which they are based. It should also be borne in mind that a number of potentially significant costs and benefits have been excluded from this analysis due to a lack of either data or a robust method existing within DWP. These include impacts on crime, health (outside of narrow NHS costs), alcohol and drug abuse as well as things like increased motivation.  These results are consistent with the wider ESF evidence base. Overall, participants have benefitted by getting outcomes at a relatively high rate. For many this has come from a low base, as they have required multiple barriers to be addressed before they can achieve some of the cashable outcomes measurable in the CBA. Qualitative research on ESF has shown that its most successful in providing wraparound support for these hardest to help individuals, which is often more costly, but potentially has some of the greatest longer-term payback.  Appendices  Appendix 1: Data sources  Data for the European Social Fund (ESF) was collected throughout the programme. This programme data (MI) includes identifiable information about participants and the date they started the programme, as well as general information about the programme. Participant contact data was also collected to enable us to match ESF data to DWP administrative data.  A wider set of characteristics are collected using administrative data. This allows for a much larger sample size (close to the population), allows variables and outcomes to be tracked over a long period.  We use administrative data from DWP, particularly from Universal Credit (UC) datasets and the National Benefits Database (NBD) for legacy benefits. These provide details of spells on DWP benefits, characteristics of DWP customers and spells on employment programmes including ESF. This is used to build up a detailed picture of an individual’s benefit history over time, which is then used in the matching.  We also use administrative data from His Majesties Revenue and Customs (HMRC) Tax System. This gives is detailed employment histories of claims and outcome measures to enable us to assess employment effects.  We combine all this information to construct an evaluation dataset containing the relevant variables for each claimant.  Appendix 2: Pseudo start dates  The European Social Fund (ESF) involved participants starting programmes at different times over several years. The benefit and employment outcomes of individual participants in our treatment group are measured weekly from the date on which they start provision. However, those in our comparison group have no natural start date from which outcomes can be measured. Therefore, it is necessary to assign a ‘pseudo-start date’ to all individuals in the control group that can be used in place of the intervention start dates.  We must ensure that these pseudo-start dates identify a period of time over which non-participants can best represent what would have happened to ESF participants had they not participated in the programme. The method we used for assigning the pseudo-start dates was to take the real start dates of the ESF participants and randomly assign them to the comparison group. These dates are assigned based on the distribution of start dates in the treatment group and the distribution of the lengths of time individuals in the treatment group had spent on benefits prior to starting provision. So within each start month (i.e. the month the individual started on the programme) the start date distributions of the comparison and treatment groups match up.  Appendix 3: Propensity Score Matching  For each participant and non-participant in the sample we compute a ‘propensity score’. This defines the probability of an individual participating on the ESF programme given their observed characteristics. We use a non-linear regression model – probit to calculate the scores, where participation is the dependent variable, and the observed characteristics are the independent variables. A full list of the variables we use in the propensity score matching is provided in Appendix 6.  After estimating the propensity score for each observation in our samples, we match each individual in the treatment group with individuals from the comparison group based on their propensity scores. We match individuals in the treatment group to many individuals in the comparison group, and the outcome of the treatment group is then controlled to the average of the matched individuals in the comparison group – this is referred to as Kernel ‘one-to-many’ matching.  For PSM to be a viable methodology, there must be sufficient common support for participants among the non-participant sample. This means we need to observe participants and non-participants with the same characteristics, so that we are effectively comparing only comparable individuals. To implement this common support, we use a bandwidth in the Kernel matching. This determines how closely the propensity score of a non-participant must be to that of a participant for selection into the final matched comparison group.  All non-participants in the sample with propensity scores within the Kernel bandwidth are selected and weighted using an Epanechnikov distribution. The bandwidth we use for our analysis is 0.0001 which although is much smaller than the standard bandwidth (0.06), it results in smaller bias and ensures a tighter match between participants and non-participants.  As part of the PSM process, each individual receives a weight. The weight assigned to non-participants depends on how many participants the non-participants match to, and how many other non-participants match to that participant. This essentially indicates their significance in the matching so that the number of weighted matched non-participant observations is equal to the number of matched participant observations.  The matching was carried out using an adaptation of the SBWA Evaluation Stata code in 2016, which relies on the Stata module psmatch2 written by Leuven and Sianesi (2003)[footnote 24]. The matched-weighted sample is then used to check the quality of the matching and to estimate the impact (see Appendix 5).  Appendix 4: Non-participant samples  In preparation for carrying out matching methods, it is necessary to define a population of non-participants, known as a comparison pool, from which the final matched control group will be selected for comparison against the treatment group. To provide a suitable comparison pool of benefit claimants who did not participate on the ESF programme, we draw samples of non-participants mirroring as closely as possible the selection criteria used to draw the participant sample.  To compare outcomes of participants and non-participants at a time period such that non-participants can represent what would have happened to ESF participants if they had not participated, we randomly assign a pseudo start date to each non-participant in a way that matches the distribution of participant start dates. The pseudo start date for non-participants is treated as equivalent to the actual start date for participants. More details of how these dates are generated are provided in Appendix 2.  For inclusion in our analysis, individuals in the comparison pool must meet the following conditions:   Those aged 19-64, in order to track some benefit history before pseudo start date Those who have a pseudo start date on or before September 2020 in order to track impacts for 3 years after pseudo start date Those who were claiming benefits at their pseudo start date, in order to have a sufficient amount of data for the matching methodology we use   Similarly to the treatment group, the comparison pool is split into three groups based on benefit type (see Table 1 for groupings).  The resulting comparison sample size is 1,035,341 participants. This is comprised of 211,813 employed benefit claimants, 222,405 unemployed benefit claimants and 601,123 inactive participants. It is important we have large comparison pools as this increases our chances of finding good matches. Values are: on programme; not on programme     Appendix 7: Assumptions for benefits calculation  The impact analysis focused on estimating the additional time on, or off, benefits and in employment as a result of ESF. It did not attempt to estimate anything around additional earnings, number of hours worked, or amount of benefit received. Therefore, it is necessary to make a number of assumptions for use in the DWP Social Cost Benefit Analysis (SCBA) model.  The analysis uses ESF participant data to establish the proportion who were subject to a health journey, the proportions in different age groups (shown in section 2) as well characteristics around number of children and regions.  For other data, the analysis assumes that all participants moving into employment are earning the NLW in 2023/24 (£10.42 per hour, when this analysis was carried out), that they were working 37 hours a week and that housing costs were in line with UK average rental costs in 2023. It is also assumed where a participant had a child, that there is only one child, and the child is over 14. Indicative analysis suggests these are broadly sensible assumptions to make but equally, they are safe assumptions that should not bias the SCBA estimates too much. For example, assuming that participants had 2 children under 14 could add around 4% to the benefit figures in each instance.
